title: VPN-Masked Cross-Border Card Testing with Sequential BIN Probing
narrative: Fraudster uses commercial VPN services to mask their true location while testing stolen card numbers from a specific country. The attacker's IP resolves to US datacenter ranges (Digital Ocean, AWS) while billing addresses cluster in Nigeria, Philippines, or Romania. Transactions show $1-3 authorization tests across 15-40 different card1 values within 90 minutes, all sharing identical P_emaildomain (typically disposable services like guerrillamail, tempmail). The dist1 feature shows 8000+ km separation between IP geolocation and billing addr1. Attacks occur during night hours (2-6 AM) in the billing country timezone but normal business hours in the IP timezone. ProductCD consistently shows 'W' (digital goods) to avoid shipping verification. Card testing succeeds at 12-18% rate before moving to larger purchases.
indicators: dist1 exceeds 8000 km between IP and billing address country
15-40 unique card1 values from single device/IP in 90-minute window
IP resolves to known VPN/datacenter ASN while addr1 in high-risk country
P_emaildomain matches disposable email services for 85%+ of attempts
TransactionAmt under $5 for initial wave, then jumps to $200-800 for successful cards
txn_hour indicates 2-6 AM in billing country timezone, 9 AM-1 PM in IP timezone
card1_txn_count_24h shows 1 for each card (first-time use pattern)
summary: A single device in a US datacenter IP address tests 15-40 different cards within 90 minutes, all with billing addresses 8000+ km away in Nigeria, Philippines, or Romania, using disposable email domains and making small $1-3 charges at night in the cardholder's timezone.
reasoning: The attacker is hiding behind a VPN to rapidly test stolen card numbers from a specific country, using tiny charges to identify which cards are still active before making larger fraudulent purchases with the validated cards.
indicator_explanations: IP location is on a different continent from the cardholder's billing address
Dozens of different cards cycle through the same device within minutes
Connection originates from commercial VPN hiding a suspicious origin country
Nearly all attempts use throwaway email addresses instead of legitimate domains
Small test charges suddenly spike to large amounts once card validates
Purchases happen during cardholder's sleep hours but attacker's business hours
Every card in the sequence is being used for the very first time
